question:
2. If \( a(x), b(x), c(x) \), and \( d(x) \) are polynomials in \( x \), show that
\[
\int_{1}^{x} a(x) c(x) d x \cdot \int_{1}^{x} b(x) d(x) d x-\int_{1}^{x} a(x) d(x) d x \cdot \int_{1}^{x} b(x) c(x) d x
\]
is divisible by \( (x-1)^{4} \).
solution:
First Solution. Since \( a, b, c, d \) are polynomials, the expression above is also a polynomial, say \( \boldsymbol{F}(\boldsymbol{x}) \). For clarity we change the variable of integration to \( t \), so that
\[
F(x)=\int_{1}^{x} a c d t \cdot \int_{1}^{x} b d d t-\int_{1}^{x} a d d t \cdot \int_{1}^{x} b c d t .
\]

It is obvious that \( F(1)=0 \), whence \( F(x) \) is divisible by \( (x-1) \). Furthermore
\[
F^{\prime}(x)=a c \int_{1}^{x} b d d t+b d \int_{1}^{x} a c d t-a d \int_{1}^{x} b c d t-b c \int_{1}^{x} a d d t,
\]
and \( F^{\prime}(1)=0 \). Also
\[
\begin{array}{c}
F^{\prime \prime}(x)=(a c)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{x} b d d t+(b d)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{x} a c d t-(a d)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{x} b c d t- \\
(b c)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{x} a d d t+a c b d+b d a c-a d b c-b c a d
\end{array}
\]
and again \( F^{\prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Finally
\[
\begin{array}{l}
F^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=(a c)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{x} b d d t+(b d)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{x} a c d t-(a d)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{x} b c d t- \\
(b c)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{x} a d d t+(a c)^{\prime} b d+(b d)^{\prime} a c-(a d)^{\prime} b c-(b c)^{\prime} a d .
\end{array}
\]

The four terms not involving an integral are seen to be \( [(a c)(b d)]^{\prime} \) \( [(a d)(b c)]^{\prime}=0 \), and again \( F^{\prime \prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Therefore \( F(x) \) is divisible by \( (x-1)^{4} \).

Second Solution. We prove a generalization. Suppose \( p, q, r \), and \( s \) are functions of class \( C^{3} \) such that \( p(u)=q(u)=r(u)=s(u)=0 \) for some fixed \( u \), and
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ll}
p^{\prime} & q^{\prime} \\
r^{\prime} & s^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|=0
\]
identically. Then
\[
f=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
p & q \\
r & s
\end{array}\right|
\]
vanishes with its first three derivatives at \( u \).
Evidently \( f(u)=0 \), and we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
f^{\prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
p^{\prime} & q^{\prime} \\
r & s
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
p & q \\
r^{\prime} & s^{\prime}
\end{array}\right| \\
f^{\prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
p^{\prime \prime} & q^{\prime \prime} \\
r & s
\end{array}\right|+2\left|\begin{array}{ll}
p^{\prime} & q^{\prime} \\
r^{\prime} & s^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
p & q \\
r^{\prime \prime} & s^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right| \\
f^{\prime \prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
p^{\prime \prime \prime} & q^{\prime \prime} \\
r & s
\end{array}\right|+3\left\{\left|\begin{array}{ll}
p^{\prime \prime} & q^{\prime \prime} \\
r^{\prime} & s^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
p^{\prime} & q^{\prime} \\
r^{\prime \prime} & s^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|\right\}+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
p & q \\
r^{\prime \prime \prime} & s^{\prime \prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|
\end{array}
\]

The first and last determinants in each expression are zero at \( u \). The middle determinant in \( f^{\prime \prime} \) is identically zero by hypothesis, and the expression in braces is the derivative of the latter determinant, so it, too, is identically zero. Therefore, \( f^{\prime}(u)=f^{\prime \prime}(u)=f^{\prime \prime \prime}(u)=0 \).

The problem posed is the special case with \( u=1 \),
\[
p=\int_{1}^{x} a c, q=\int_{1}^{x} a d, r=\int_{1}^{x} b c, s=\int_{1}^{x} b d .
\]

Third Solution. A solution using linear algebra can be given. The mapping
\[
\phi:(a, b, c, d) \mapsto F
\]
is a multilinear map \( P^{4} \rightarrow P \) where \( P \) is the space of polynomials. Since the set of polynomials divisible by \( (x-1)^{4} \) is a linear subspace of \( P \), it is sufficient to verify the result as the given polynomials \( a, b, c \), and \( d \) vary over a basis of \( P \).
Take the basis \( 1,(x-1),(x-1)^{2}, \ldots \). Then if \( a(x)=(x-1)^{p} \), \( b(x)=(x-1)^{q}, c(x)=(x-1)^{r} \), and \( d(x)=(x-1)^{s} \), we have \( \phi(a, b \), \( c, d)=F \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(x) & =(x-1)^{p+q+r+s+2} \times \\
& {\left[\frac{1}{p+r+1} \cdot \frac{1}{q+s+1}-\frac{1}{p+s+1} \cdot \frac{1}{q+r+1}\right] . }
\end{aligned}
\]

If \( p+q+r+s \geq 2 \), then clearly \( F(x) \) is divisible by \( (x-1)^{4} \). If \( p+q+r+s<2 \), either all the exponents are zero or all but one are. In each of these cases, the bracketed expression in (1) vanishes, so \( F(x)=0 \) and is divisible by \( (x-1)^{4} \).

descriptive_long.question:
2. If polyone(independ), polytwo(independ), polythr(independ), and polyfour(independ) are polynomials in independ, show that
\[
\int_{1}^{independ} polyone(independ) \, polythr(independ) \, d independ \cdot \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo(independ) \, polyfour(independ) \, d independ-\int_{1}^{independ} polyone(independ) \, polyfour(independ) \, d independ \cdot \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo(independ) \, polythr(independ) \, d independ
\]
is divisible by \( (independ-1)^{4} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Since polyone, polytwo, polythr, polyfour are polynomials, the expression above is also a polynomial, say \boldsymbol{polyfunc}(\boldsymbol{independ}). For clarity we change the variable of integration to integvar, so that
\[
polyfunc(independ)=
\left( \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \, polythr \, d \integvar \right)
\left( \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \, polyfour \, d \integvar \right)
-
\left( \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \, polyfour \, d \integvar \right)
\left( \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \, polythr \, d \integvar \right).
\]

It is obvious that \( polyfunc(1)=0 \), whence \( polyfunc(independ) \) is divisible by \( (independ-1) \). Furthermore
\[
polyfunc^{\prime}(independ)=
polyone \; polythr \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \; polyfour \, d \integvar
+
polytwo \; polyfour \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \; polythr \, d \integvar
-
polyone \; polyfour \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \; polythr \, d \integvar
-
polytwo \; polythr \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \; polyfour \, d \integvar ,
\]
and \( polyfunc^{\prime}(1)=0 \). Also
\[
\begin{aligned}
polyfunc^{\prime \prime}(independ)=&
(polyone \; polythr)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \; polyfour \, d \integvar
+
(polytwo \; polyfour)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \; polythr \, d \integvar \\
&-
(polyone \; polyfour)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \; polythr \, d \integvar
-
(polytwo \; polythr)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \; polyfour \, d \integvar \\
&+
polyone \; polythr \; polytwo \; polyfour
+
polytwo \; polyfour \; polyone \; polythr
-
polyone \; polyfour \; polytwo \; polythr
-
polytwo \; polythr \; polyone \; polyfour ,
\end{aligned}
\]
and again \( polyfunc^{\prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Finally
\[
\begin{aligned}
polyfunc^{\prime \prime \prime}(independ)=&
(polyone \; polythr)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \; polyfour \, d \integvar
+
(polytwo \; polyfour)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \; polythr \, d \integvar \\
&-
(polyone \; polyfour)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \; polythr \, d \integvar
-
(polytwo \; polythr)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{independ} polyone \; polyfour \, d \integvar \\
&+
(polyone \; polythr)^{\prime} \; polytwo \; polyfour
+
(polytwo \; polyfour)^{\prime} \; polyone \; polythr \\
&-
(polyone \; polyfour)^{\prime} \; polytwo \; polythr
-
(polytwo \; polythr)^{\prime} \; polyone \; polyfour .
\end{aligned}
\]

The four terms not involving an integral are seen to be 
\(
[(polyone \; polythr)(polytwo \; polyfour)]^{\prime}-[(polyone \; polyfour)(polytwo \; polythr)]^{\prime}=0,
\)
and again \( polyfunc^{\prime \prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Therefore \( polyfunc(independ) \) is divisible by \( (independ-1)^{4} \).

Second Solution. We prove a generalization. Suppose funcone, functwo, functhr, and funcfour are functions of class \( classsym^{3} \) such that \( funcone(anchorpt)=functwo(anchorpt)=functhr(anchorpt)=funcfour(anchorpt)=0 \) for some fixed anchorpt, and
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ll}
funcone^{\prime} & functwo^{\prime} \\
functhr^{\prime} & funcfour^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|=0
\]
identically. Then
\[
detfunc=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
funcone & functwo \\
functhr & funcfour
\end{array}\right|
\]
vanishes with its first three derivatives at anchorpt.
Evidently \( detfunc(anchorpt)=0 \), and we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
detfunc^{\prime}&=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
funcone^{\prime} & functwo^{\prime} \\
functhr & funcfour
\end{array}\right|
+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
funcone & functwo \\
functhr^{\prime} & funcfour^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|,\\
detfunc^{\prime \prime}&=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
funcone^{\prime \prime} & functwo^{\prime \prime} \\
functhr & funcfour
\end{array}\right|
+2\left|\begin{array}{ll}
funcone^{\prime} & functwo^{\prime} \\
functhr^{\prime} & funcfour^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|
+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
funcone & functwo \\
functhr^{\prime \prime} & funcfour^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|,\\
detfunc^{\prime \prime \prime}&=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
funcone^{\prime \prime \prime} & functwo^{\prime \prime \prime} \\
functhr & funcfour
\end{array}\right|
+3\left\{
\left|\begin{array}{ll}
funcone^{\prime \prime} & functwo^{\prime \prime} \\
functhr^{\prime} & funcfour^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|
+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
funcone^{\prime} & functwo^{\prime} \\
functhr^{\prime \prime} & funcfour^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|
\right\}
+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
funcone & functwo \\
functhr^{\prime \prime \prime} & funcfour^{\prime \prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|.
\end{aligned}
\]

The first and last determinants in each expression are zero at anchorpt. The middle determinant in \( detfunc^{\prime \prime} \) is identically zero by hypothesis, and the expression in braces is the derivative of the latter determinant, so it, too, is identically zero. Therefore, \( detfunc^{\prime}(anchorpt)=detfunc^{\prime \prime}(anchorpt)=detfunc^{\prime \prime \prime}(anchorpt)=0 \).

The problem posed is the special case with \( anchorpt=1 \),
\[
funcone=\int_{1}^{independ} polyone \, polythr, \qquad
functwo=\int_{1}^{independ} polyone \, polyfour, \qquad
functhr=\int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \, polythr, \qquad
funcfour=\int_{1}^{independ} polytwo \, polyfour .
\]

Third Solution. A solution using linear algebra can be given. The mapping
\[
\operatorname{linearphi}:(polyone, polytwo, polythr, polyfour) \mapsto polyfunc
\]
is a multilinear map \( polyspace^{4} \rightarrow polyspace \) where \( polyspace \) is the space of polynomials. Since the set of polynomials divisible by \( (independ-1)^{4} \) is a linear subspace of \( polyspace \), it is sufficient to verify the result as the given polynomials polyone, polytwo, polythr, and polyfour vary over a basis of \( polyspace \).

Take the basis \( 1,(independ-1),(independ-1)^{2}, \ldots \). Then if \( polyone(independ)=(independ-1)^{funcone}, \; polytwo(independ)=(independ-1)^{functwo}, \; polythr(independ)=(independ-1)^{functhr}, \; polyfour(independ)=(independ-1)^{funcfour}, \) we have \( \operatorname{linearphi}(polyone, polytwo, polythr, polyfour)=polyfunc \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
polyfunc(independ) & =(independ-1)^{funcone+functwo+functhr+funcfour+2} \times \\
& \left[\frac{1}{funcone+functhr+1} \cdot \frac{1}{functwo+funcfour+1}-\frac{1}{funcone+funcfour+1} \cdot \frac{1}{functwo+functhr+1}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

If \( funcone+functwo+functhr+funcfour \geq 2 \), then clearly \( polyfunc(independ) \) is divisible by \( (independ-1)^{4} \). If \( funcone+functwo+functhr+funcfour<2 \), either all the exponents are zero or all but one are. In each of these cases, the bracketed expression above vanishes, so \( polyfunc(independ)=0 \) and is divisible by \( (independ-1)^{4} \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. If \( hucklebee(marbleton), luminance(marbleton), quartzite(marbleton), and silhouette(marbleton) \) are polynomials in \( marbleton \), show that
\[
\int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee(marbleton)\, quartzite(marbleton)\, d marbleton \cdot \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance(marbleton)\, silhouette(marbleton)\, d marbleton-\int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee(marbleton)\, silhouette(marbleton)\, d marbleton \cdot \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance(marbleton)\, quartzite(marbleton)\, d marbleton
\]
is divisible by \( (marbleton-1)^{4} \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Since \( hucklebee, luminance, quartzite, silhouette \) are polynomials, the expression above is also a polynomial, say \( \boldsymbol{serendity}(\boldsymbol{marbleton}) \). For clarity we change the variable of integration to \( harmonica \), so that
\[
serendity(marbleton)=\int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, quartzite\, d harmonica \cdot \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, silhouette\, d harmonica-\int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, silhouette\, d harmonica \cdot \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, quartzite\, d harmonica .
\]

It is obvious that \( serendity(1)=0 \), whence \( serendity(marbleton) \) is divisible by \( (marbleton-1) \). Furthermore
\[
serendity^{\prime}(marbleton)=hucklebee\, quartzite \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, silhouette\, d harmonica+luminance\, silhouette \int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, quartzite\, d harmonica-hucklebee\, silhouette \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, quartzite\, d harmonica-luminance\, quartzite \int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, silhouette\, d harmonica,
\]
and \( serendity^{\prime}(1)=0 \). Also
\[
\begin{array}{c}
serendity^{\prime \prime}(marbleton)=(hucklebee\, quartzite)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, silhouette\, d harmonica+(luminance\, silhouette)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, quartzite\, d harmonica-(hucklebee\, silhouette)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, quartzite\, d harmonica-\\
(luminance\, quartzite)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, silhouette\, d harmonica+hucklebee\, quartzite\, luminance\, silhouette+luminance\, silhouette\, hucklebee\, quartzite-hucklebee\, silhouette\, luminance\, quartzite-luminance\, quartzite\, hucklebee\, silhouette\end{array}
\]
and again \( serendity^{\prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Finally
\[
\begin{array}{l}
serendity^{\prime \prime \prime}(marbleton)=(hucklebee\, quartzite)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, silhouette\, d harmonica+(luminance\, silhouette)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, quartzite\, d harmonica-(hucklebee\, silhouette)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, quartzite\, d harmonica-\\
(luminance\, quartzite)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, silhouette\, d harmonica+(hucklebee\, quartzite)^{\prime}\, luminance\, silhouette+(luminance\, silhouette)^{\prime}\, hucklebee\, quartzite-(hucklebee\, silhouette)^{\prime}\, luminance\, quartzite-(luminance\, quartzite)^{\prime}\, hucklebee\, silhouette .\end{array}
\]

The four terms not involving an integral are seen to be \( [(hucklebee\, quartzite)(luminance\, silhouette)]^{\prime} \) \( [(hucklebee\, silhouette)(luminance\, quartzite)]^{\prime}=0 \), and again \( serendity^{\prime \prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Therefore \( serendity(marbleton) \) is divisible by \( (marbleton-1)^{4} \).

Second Solution. We prove a generalization. Suppose \( tangerine, windchime, pendulous, nightfall \) are functions of class \( longitude^{3} \) such that \( tangerine(caterpillar)=windchime(caterpillar)=pendulous(caterpillar)=nightfall(caterpillar)=0 \) for some fixed \( caterpillar \), and
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ll}
tangerine^{\prime} & windchime^{\prime} \\
pendulous^{\prime} & nightfall^{\prime}\end{array}\right|=0
\]
identically. Then
\[
meadowlark=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
tangerine & windchime \\
pendulous & nightfall\end{array}\right|
\]
vanishes with its first three derivatives at \( caterpillar \).
Evidently \( meadowlark(caterpillar)=0 \), and we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
meadowlark^{\prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
tangerine^{\prime} & windchime^{\prime} \\
pendulous & nightfall\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
tangerine & windchime \\
pendulous^{\prime} & nightfall^{\prime}\end{array}\right| \\
meadowlark^{\prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
tangerine^{\prime \prime} & windchime^{\prime \prime} \\
pendulous & nightfall\end{array}\right|+2\left|\begin{array}{ll}
tangerine^{\prime} & windchime^{\prime} \\
pendulous^{\prime} & nightfall^{\prime}\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
tangerine & windchime \\
pendulous^{\prime \prime} & nightfall^{\prime \prime}\end{array}\right| \\
meadowlark^{\prime \prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
tangerine^{\prime \prime \prime} & windchime^{\prime \prime} \\
pendulous & nightfall\end{array}\right|+3\left\{\left|\begin{array}{ll}
tangerine^{\prime \prime} & windchime^{\prime \prime} \\
pendulous^{\prime} & nightfall^{\prime}\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
tangerine^{\prime} & windchime^{\prime} \\
pendulous^{\prime \prime} & nightfall^{\prime \prime}\end{array}\right|\right\}+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
tangerine & windchime \\
pendulous^{\prime \prime \prime} & nightfall^{\prime \prime \prime}\end{array}\right|
\end{array}
\]

The first and last determinants in each expression are zero at \( caterpillar \). The middle determinant in \( meadowlark^{\prime \prime} \) is identically zero by hypothesis, and the expression in braces is the derivative of the latter determinant, so it, too, is identically zero. Therefore, \( meadowlark^{\prime}(caterpillar)=meadowlark^{\prime \prime}(caterpillar)=meadowlark^{\prime \prime \prime}(caterpillar)=0 \).

The problem posed is the special case with \( caterpillar=1 \),
\[
tangerine=\int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, quartzite, \quad windchime=\int_{1}^{marbleton} hucklebee\, silhouette, \quad pendulous=\int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, quartzite, \quad nightfall=\int_{1}^{marbleton} luminance\, silhouette .\]

Third Solution. A solution using linear algebra can be given. The mapping
\[
\gondolier:(hucklebee, luminance, quartzite, silhouette) \mapsto serendity
\]
is a multilinear map \( crossroad^{4} \rightarrow crossroad \) where \( crossroad \) is the space of polynomials. Since the set of polynomials divisible by \( (marbleton-1)^{4} \) is a linear subspace of \( crossroad \), it is sufficient to verify the result as the given polynomials \( hucklebee, luminance, quartzite \), and \( silhouette \) vary over a basis of \( crossroad \).
Take the basis \( 1,(marbleton-1),(marbleton-1)^{2}, \ldots \). Then if \( hucklebee(marbleton)=(marbleton-1)^{tangerine}, \; luminance(marbleton)=(marbleton-1)^{windchime},\; quartzite(marbleton)=(marbleton-1)^{pendulous}, \) and \( silhouette(marbleton)=(marbleton-1)^{nightfall} \), we have \( \gondolier(hucklebee, luminance, quartzite, silhouette)=serendity \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
serendity(marbleton) & =(marbleton-1)^{tangerine+windchime+pendulous+nightfall+2} \times \\
& \left[\frac{1}{tangerine+pendulous+1} \cdot \frac{1}{windchime+nightfall+1}-\frac{1}{tangerine+nightfall+1} \cdot \frac{1}{windchime+pendulous+1}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

If \( tangerine+windchime+pendulous+nightfall \ge 2 \), then clearly \( serendity(marbleton) \) is divisible by \( (marbleton-1)^{4} \). If \( tangerine+windchime+pendulous+nightfall<2 \), either all the exponents are zero or all but one are. In each of these cases, the bracketed expression in (1) vanishes, so \( serendity(marbleton)=0 \) and is divisible by \( (marbleton-1)^{4} \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. If \( transcend(knownvalue), irrational(knownvalue), constant(knownvalue), staticity(knownvalue) \) are polynomials in \( knownvalue \), show that
\[
\int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend(knownvalue)\, constant(knownvalue)\, d knownvalue \cdot \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational(knownvalue)\, staticity(knownvalue)\, d knownvalue-\int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend(knownvalue)\, staticity(knownvalue)\, d knownvalue \cdot \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational(knownvalue)\, constant(knownvalue)\, d knownvalue
\]
is divisible by \( (knownvalue-1)^{4} \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. Since \( transcend, irrational, constant, staticity \) are polynomials, the expression above is also a polynomial, say \( \boldsymbol{simplicity}(\boldsymbol{knownvalue}) \). For clarity we change the variable of integration to \( motionless \), so that
\[
simplicity(knownvalue)=\int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, constant\, d motionless \cdot \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, staticity\, d motionless-\int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, staticity\, d motionless \cdot \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, constant\, d motionless .
\]

It is obvious that \( simplicity(1)=0 \), whence \( simplicity(knownvalue) \) is divisible by \( (knownvalue-1) \). Furthermore
\[
simplicity^{\prime}(knownvalue)=transcend\, constant \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, staticity\, d motionless+irrational\, staticity \int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, constant\, d motionless-transcend\, staticity \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, constant\, d motionless-irrational\, constant \int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, staticity\, d motionless,
\]
and \( simplicity^{\prime}(1)=0 \). Also
\[
\begin{array}{c}
simplicity^{\prime \prime}(knownvalue)=(transcend\, constant)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, staticity\, d motionless+(irrational\, staticity)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, constant\, d motionless-(transcend\, staticity)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, constant\, d motionless- \\
(irrational\, constant)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, staticity\, d motionless+transcend\, constant\, irrational\, staticity+irrational\, staticity\, transcend\, constant-transcend\, staticity\, irrational\, constant-irrational\, constant\, transcend\, staticity
\end{array}
\]
and again \( simplicity^{\prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Finally
\[
\begin{array}{l}
simplicity^{\prime \prime \prime}(knownvalue)=(transcend\, constant)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, staticity\, d motionless+(irrational\, staticity)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, constant\, d motionless-(transcend\, staticity)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, constant\, d motionless- \\
(irrational\, constant)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, staticity\, d motionless+(transcend\, constant)^{\prime}\, irrational\, staticity+(irrational\, staticity)^{\prime}\, transcend\, constant-(transcend\, staticity)^{\prime}\, irrational\, constant-(irrational\, constant)^{\prime}\, transcend\, staticity .
\end{array}
\]

The four terms not involving an integral are seen to be \( [(transcend\, constant)(irrational\, staticity)]^{\prime} [(transcend\, staticity)(irrational\, constant)]^{\prime}=0 \), and again \( simplicity^{\prime \prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Therefore \( simplicity(knownvalue) \) is divisible by \( (knownvalue-1)^{4} \).

Second Solution. We prove a generalization. Suppose \( discretefn, unstablefn, sporadicfn \), and \( chaoticfn \) are functions of class \( disjointset^{3} \) such that \( discretefn(mutablept)=unstablefn(mutablept)=sporadicfn(mutablept)=chaoticfn(mutablept)=0 \) for some fixed \( mutablept \), and
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ll}
discretefn^{\prime} & unstablefn^{\prime} \\
sporadicfn^{\prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|=0
\]
identically. Then
\[
fixednumber=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
discretefn & unstablefn \\
sporadicfn & chaoticfn
\end{array}\right|
\]
vanishes with its first three derivatives at \( mutablept \).
Evidently \( fixednumber(mutablept)=0 \), and we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
fixednumber^{\prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
discretefn^{\prime} & unstablefn^{\prime} \\
sporadicfn & chaoticfn
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
discretefn & unstablefn \\
sporadicfn^{\prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime}
\end{array}\right| \\
fixednumber^{\prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
discretefn^{\prime \prime} & unstablefn^{\prime \prime} \\
sporadicfn & chaoticfn
\end{array}\right|+2\left|\begin{array}{ll}
discretefn^{\prime} & unstablefn^{\prime} \\
sporadicfn^{\prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
discretefn & unstablefn \\
sporadicfn^{\prime \prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right| \\
fixednumber^{\prime \prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
discretefn^{\prime \prime \prime} & unstablefn^{\prime \prime} \\
sporadicfn & chaoticfn
\end{array}\right|+3\left\{\left|\begin{array}{ll}
discretefn^{\prime \prime} & unstablefn^{\prime \prime} \\
sporadicfn^{\prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
discretefn^{\prime} & unstablefn^{\prime} \\
sporadicfn^{\prime \prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|\right\}+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
discretefn & unstablefn \\
sporadicfn^{\prime \prime \prime} & chaoticfn^{\prime \prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|
\end{array}
\]

The first and last determinants in each expression are zero at \( mutablept \). The middle determinant in \( fixednumber^{\prime \prime} \) is identically zero by hypothesis, and the expression in braces is the derivative of the latter determinant, so it, too, is identically zero. Therefore, \( fixednumber^{\prime}(mutablept)=fixednumber^{\prime \prime}(mutablept)=fixednumber^{\prime \prime \prime}(mutablept)=0 \).

The problem posed is the special case with \( mutablept=1 \),
\[
discretefn=\int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, constant, \quad unstablefn=\int_{1}^{knownvalue} transcend\, staticity, \quad sporadicfn=\int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, constant, \quad chaoticfn=\int_{1}^{knownvalue} irrational\, staticity .
\]

Third Solution. A solution using linear algebra can be given. The mapping
\[
voidmapping:(transcend, irrational, constant, staticity) \mapsto simplicity
\]
is a multilinear map \( nonpolyset^{4} \rightarrow nonpolyset \) where \( nonpolyset \) is the space of polynomials. Since the set of polynomials divisible by \( (knownvalue-1)^{4} \) is a linear subspace of \( nonpolyset \), it is sufficient to verify the result as the given polynomials \( transcend, irrational, constant, staticity \) vary over a basis of \( nonpolyset \).
Take the basis \( 1,(knownvalue-1),(knownvalue-1)^{2}, \ldots \). Then if \( transcend(knownvalue)=(knownvalue-1)^{discretefn}, irrational(knownvalue)=(knownvalue-1)^{unstablefn}, constant(knownvalue)=(knownvalue-1)^{sporadicfn} \), and \( staticity(knownvalue)=(knownvalue-1)^{chaoticfn} \), we have \( voidmapping(transcend, irrational, constant, staticity)=simplicity \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
simplicity(knownvalue) & =(knownvalue-1)^{discretefn+unstablefn+sporadicfn+chaoticfn+2} \times \\
& {\left[\frac{1}{discretefn+sporadicfn+1} \cdot \frac{1}{unstablefn+chaoticfn+1}-\frac{1}{discretefn+chaoticfn+1} \cdot \frac{1}{unstablefn+sporadicfn+1}\right] . }
\end{aligned}
\]

If \( discretefn+unstablefn+sporadicfn+chaoticfn \geq 2 \), then clearly \( simplicity(knownvalue) \) is divisible by \( (knownvalue-1)^{4} \). If \( discretefn+unstablefn+sporadicfn+chaoticfn<2 \), either all the exponents are zero or all but one are. In each of these cases, the bracketed expression in (1) vanishes, so \( simplicity(knownvalue)=0 \) and is divisible by \( (knownvalue-1)^{4} \).

garbled_string.question:
2. If \( klmnprstuv(qzxwvtnp), wxyzabcdet(qzxwvtnp), opqrstuvwx(qzxwvtnp), \) and \( yzabcdefgi(qzxwvtnp) \) are polynomials in \( qzxwvtnp \), show that
\[
\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv(qzxwvtnp) opqrstuvwx(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp \cdot \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet(qzxwvtnp) yzabcdefgi(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp-\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv(qzxwvtnp) yzabcdefgi(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp \cdot \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet(qzxwvtnp) opqrstuvwx(qzxwvtnp) d qzxwvtnp
\]
is divisible by \( (qzxwvtnp-1)^{4} \).
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. Since \( klmnprstuv, wxyzabcdet, opqrstuvwx, yzabcdefgi \) are polynomials, the expression above is also a polynomial, say \( \boldsymbol{lmnoprqs}(\boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp}) \). For clarity we change the variable of integration to \( hjgrksla \), so that
\[
lmnoprqs(qzxwvtnp)=\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla \cdot \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla-\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla \cdot \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla .
\]

It is obvious that \( lmnoprqs(1)=0 \), whence \( lmnoprqs(qzxwvtnp) \) is divisible by \( (qzxwvtnp-1) \). Furthermore
\[
lmnoprqs^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)=klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla+wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla-klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla-wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla,
\]
and \( lmnoprqs^{\prime}(1)=0 \). Also
\[
\begin{array}{c}
lmnoprqs^{\prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)=(klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla+(wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla-(klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla- \\
(wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx)^{\prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla+klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi+wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx-klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx-wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi
\end{array}
\]
and again \( lmnoprqs^{\prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Finally
\[
\begin{array}{l}
lmnoprqs^{\prime \prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)=(klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla+(wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla-(klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx d hjgrksla- \\
(wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx)^{\prime \prime} \int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi d hjgrksla+(klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx)^{\prime} wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi+(wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi)^{\prime} klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx-(klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi)^{\prime} wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx-(wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx)^{\prime} klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi .
\end{array}
\]

The four terms not involving an integral are seen to be \( [(klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx)(wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi)]^{\prime} \) \( [(klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi)(wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx)]^{\prime}=0 \), and again \( lmnoprqs^{\prime \prime \prime}(1)=0 \). Therefore \( lmnoprqs(qzxwvtnp) \) is divisible by \( (qzxwvtnp-1)^{4} \).

Second Solution. We prove a generalization. Suppose \( hijklmnopq, rstuvwxyza, bcdefghijk, \) and \( lmnopqrstuv \) are functions of class \( selkruwqop^{3} \) such that \( hijklmnopq(cdefghij)=rstuvwxyza(cdefghij)=bcdefghijk(cdefghij)=lmnopqrstuv(cdefghij)=0 \) for some fixed \( cdefghij \), and
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ll}
hijklmnopq^{\prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime} \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|=0
\]
identically. Then
\[
tuvxyzab=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
hijklmnopq & rstuvwxyza \\
bcdefghijk & lmnopqrstuv
\end{array}\right|
\]
vanishes with its first three derivatives at \( cdefghij \).
Evidently \( tuvxyzab(cdefghij)=0 \), and we have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
tuvxyzab^{\prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
hijklmnopq^{\prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime} \\
bcdefghijk & lmnopqrstuv
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
hijklmnopq & rstuvwxyza \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime}
\end{array}\right| \\
tuvxyzab^{\prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
hijklmnopq^{\prime \prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime \prime} \\
bcdefghijk & lmnopqrstuv
\end{array}\right|+2\left|\begin{array}{ll}
hijklmnopq^{\prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime} \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
hijklmnopq & rstuvwxyza \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime \prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right| \\
tuvxyzab^{\prime \prime \prime}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
hijklmnopq^{\prime \prime \prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime \prime} \\
bcdefghijk & lmnopqrstuv
\end{array}\right|+3\left\{\left|\begin{array}{ll}
hijklmnopq^{\prime \prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime \prime} \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
hijklmnopq^{\prime} & rstuvwxyza^{\prime} \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime \prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|\right\}+\left|\begin{array}{cc}
hijklmnopq & rstuvwxyza \\
bcdefghijk^{\prime \prime \prime} & lmnopqrstuv^{\prime \prime \prime}
\end{array}\right|
\end{array}
\]

The first and last determinants in each expression are zero at \( cdefghij \). The middle determinant in \( tuvxyzab^{\prime \prime} \) is identically zero by hypothesis, and the expression in braces is the derivative of the latter determinant, so it, too, is identically zero. Therefore, \( tuvxyzab^{\prime}(cdefghij)=tuvxyzab^{\prime \prime}(cdefghij)=tuvxyzab^{\prime \prime \prime}(cdefghij)=0 \).

The problem posed is the special case with \( cdefghij=1 \),
\[
hijklmnopq=\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv opqrstuvwx, \quad rstuvwxyza=\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} klmnprstuv yzabcdefgi, \quad bcdefghijk=\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet opqrstuvwx, \quad lmnopqrstuv=\int_{1}^{qzxwvtnp} wxyzabcdet yzabcdefgi .
\]

Third Solution. A solution using linear algebra can be given. The mapping
\[
ngrhdktps:(klmnprstuv, wxyzabcdet, opqrstuvwx, yzabcdefgi) \mapsto lmnoprqs
\]
is a multilinear map \( alqkzmxncb^{4} \rightarrow alqkzmxncb \) where \( alqkzmxncb \) is the space of polynomials. Since the set of polynomials divisible by \( (qzxwvtnp-1)^{4} \) is a linear subspace of \( alqkzmxncb \), it is sufficient to verify the result as the given polynomials \( klmnprstuv, wxyzabcdet, opqrstuvwx, \) and \( yzabcdefgi \) vary over a basis of \( alqkzmxncb \).
Take the basis \( 1,(qzxwvtnp-1),(qzxwvtnp-1)^{2}, \ldots \). Then if \( klmnprstuv(qzxwvtnp)=(qzxwvtnp-1)^{hijklmnopq}, wxyzabcdet(qzxwvtnp)=(qzxwvtnp-1)^{rstuvwxyza}, opqrstuvwx(qzxwvtnp)=(qzxwvtnp-1)^{bcdefghijk} \), and \( yzabcdefgi(qzxwvtnp)=(qzxwvtnp-1)^{lmnopqrstuv} \), we have \( ngrhdktps(klmnprstuv, wxyzabcdet, opqrstuvwx, yzabcdefgi)=lmnoprqs \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
lmnoprqs(qzxwvtnp) & =(qzxwvtnp-1)^{hijklmnopq+rstuvwxyza+bcdefghijk+lmnopqrstuv+2} \times \\
& {\left[\frac{1}{hijklmnopq+bcdefghijk+1} \cdot \frac{1}{rstuvwxyza+lmnopqrstuv+1}-\frac{1}{hijklmnopq+lmnopqrstuv+1} \cdot \frac{1}{rstuvwxyza+bcdefghijk+1}\right] . }
\end{aligned}
\]

If \( hijklmnopq+rstuvwxyza+bcdefghijk+lmnopqrstuv \geq 2 \), then clearly \( lmnoprqs(qzxwvtnp) \) is divisible by \( (qzxwvtnp-1)^{4} \). If \( hijklmnopq+rstuvwxyza+bcdefghijk+lmnopqrstuv<2 \), either all the exponents are zero or all but one are. In each of these cases, the bracketed expression in (1) vanishes, so \( lmnoprqs(qzxwvtnp)=0 \) and is divisible by \( (qzxwvtnp-1)^{4} \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  

\[
a_{1}(x),\;a_{2}(x),\;a_{3}(x)\qquad\text{and}\qquad  
b_{1}(x),\;b_{2}(x),\;b_{3}(x)
\]

be real-analytic functions on an open interval \(I\subset\mathbb{R}\) that
contains the point \(\pi\).
For an analytic function \(f\) put  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}f\;:=\;
\min\Bigl\{m\ge 0\;\Bigm|\;
\dfrac{f^{(m)}(\pi)}{m!}\neq 0\Bigr\},
\qquad
\bigl(\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}0:=+\infty\bigr).
\]

Assume that the triple \((b_{1},b_{2},b_{3})\) is \emph{not}
identically zero and set  

\[
k:=\min\Bigl\{\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{1},\;
              \operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{2},\;
              \operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{3}\Bigr\}.
\]
After a possible permutation we may (and do) suppose  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{1}=k. \tag{\(\diamondsuit\)}
\]

For \(x\in I\) define the \(3\times3\) matrix  

\[
M(x)=\bigl(m_{ij}(x)\bigr)_{1\le i,j\le 3},
\qquad
m_{ij}(x):=\int_{\pi}^{x} a_{i}(t)\,b_{j}(t)\,dt ,
\]
and denote \(\displaystyle\Delta(x):=\det M(x)\).

a)  Prove the \emph{universal} estimate  

\[
\boxed{\;\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta\;\ge\;3k+7\;} . \tag{\(*\)}
\]

b)  Show that the bound \((*)\) is optimal:  
for every fixed \(k\in\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}\) there exist analytic sextuples  

\[
\bigl(a_{1},a_{2},a_{3};\,b_{1},b_{2},b_{3}\bigr)\qquad
\text{with}\quad
\min_{j}\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{j}=k
\]
such that  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7 .
\]

Consequently, when \(k=0\) the determinant \(\Delta(x)\) is always
divisible by \((x-\pi)^{7}\), and the exponent \(7\) cannot be reduced.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

\[
\varepsilon:=x-\pi,\qquad 
\tau:=t-\pi,
\]
and use the \(O(\,\cdot\,)\)-notation component-wise for vectors and
matrices.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a) - Proof of the inequality \((*)\).

Step 0.  Taylor expansions.  
Write  

\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{j}(t)&=\tau^{k}\sum_{r\ge 0}\beta_{j,r}\,\tau^{r},
&\qquad& \beta_{1,0}\neq 0\;(\text{by }(\diamondsuit)),\\[2mm]
a_{i}(t)&=\sum_{s\ge 0}\alpha_{i,s}\,\tau^{s},
\qquad 1\le i\le 3.
\end{aligned}
\]
Put  

\[
s:=\min\Bigl\{s\ge 0\mid\exists\,i:\alpha_{i,s}\neq 0\Bigr\},\qquad 
\widehat u:=(\alpha_{1,s},\alpha_{2,s},\alpha_{3,s})^{\mathsf T}\neq 0 .
\]

Step 1.  The first column \(C_{1}\).  
For \(j=1\) the integrand is \(\tau^{k}\tau^{s}= \tau^{k+s}\); hence  

\[
C_{1}:=
\begin{bmatrix}m_{11}\\m_{21}\\m_{31}\end{bmatrix}
=\varepsilon^{k+1+s}\bigl(\widehat u+\varepsilon u_{1}+O(\varepsilon^{2})\bigr). \tag{1}
\]

Step 2.  Raising the order of the remaining columns.  
Put \(\lambda_{j}:=\beta_{j,0}/\beta_{1,0}\;(j=2,3)\) and perform the
elementary column operations \(C_{j}\longleftarrow C_{j}-\lambda_{j}C_{1}\).
Because \(b_{j}-\lambda_{j}b_{1}\) vanishes to order \(k+1\) at
\(t=\pi\), the transformed columns satisfy  

\[
C_{j}= \varepsilon^{k+2+s}\bigl(v_{j}+\varepsilon w_{j}+O(\varepsilon^{2})\bigr),
\qquad v_{j}=c_{j}\,\widehat u,\;c_{j}\in\mathbb{R}. \tag{2}
\]

Step 3.  Extracting the obvious overall power of \(\varepsilon\).  
With (1)-(2) we can factor  

\[
\Delta(x)=\varepsilon^{3k+5+3s}\,
B(\varepsilon),
\]
where  

\[
B(\varepsilon):=
\det\!\bigl[\widehat u+\varepsilon u_{1}+O(\varepsilon^{2}),
               \;v_{2}+\varepsilon w_{2}+O(\varepsilon^{2}),
               \;v_{3}+\varepsilon w_{3}+O(\varepsilon^{2})\bigr]. \tag{3}
\]

Step 4.  Vanishing of the constant and linear terms in \(B\).  
At \(\varepsilon=0\) the three column-vectors inside the determinant in
(3) are all multiples of \(\widehat u\), hence the determinant vanishes.  
Because each of the two vectors \(v_{2},v_{3}\) is a multiple of the
first one, every first-order term in the expansion of \(B(\varepsilon)\)
still contains two proportional columns and therefore also vanishes.
Consequently  

\[
B(\varepsilon)=\varepsilon^{2}\,B_{2}+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{3}\bigr) \qquad(\varepsilon\to 0). \tag{4}
\]

Inequalities (3)-(4) give  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta
\;\ge\;3k+5+3s+2
\;=\;3k+7+3s
\;\ge\;3k+7 ,
\]

which is exactly the universal estimate \((*)\).
(Note that when \(s>0\) the obtained bound is in fact stronger.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Optional remark on the coefficient \(B_{2}\).  
Formula (4) shows that \(\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta\) will jump to at
least \(3k+8+3s\) precisely when the scalar \(B_{2}\) vanishes.  One can
check that \(B_{2}\) is a non-trivial analytic function of the
coefficients \(\{\alpha_{i,r}\},\{\beta_{j,r}\}\); hence \(B_{2}=0\) is a
\emph{codimension-$1$} condition in the space of analytic sextuples.
For a ``generic'' choice of the six functions one therefore has
\(B_{2}\neq 0\) and the stronger bound
\(\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7+3s\).  This observation is not
needed for part (a), but it explains why the equality examples in part
(b) (which have \(s=0\)) do realise the exact exponent \(3k+7\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b) - Sharpness of the bound \((*)\).

Fix \(k\ge 0\) and take  

\[
\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
a_{1}(t)&:=1,\\
a_{2}(t)&:=1+\tau,\\
a_{3}(t)&:=\tau,\\
b_{1}(t)&:=\tau^{k},\\
b_{2}(t)&:=\tau^{k}\bigl(1+\tau\bigr),\\
b_{3}(t)&:=\tau^{k+1}\bigl(1+\tau\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{5}
\]
Here \(s=0\) and \(\min_{j}\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{j}=k\); condition
\((\diamondsuit)\) is satisfied.

A straightforward (though routine) Taylor computation of the nine
integrals shows  

\[
\Delta(\pi+\varepsilon)=
\frac{k+3}{12(k+1)(k+2)}\,\varepsilon^{3k+7}
\,+\,O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{3k+8}\bigr),\qquad (\varepsilon\to 0). \tag{6}
\]
Since the leading coefficient in (6) is
\(\displaystyle\frac{k+3}{12(k+1)(k+2)}\neq 0\) for every \(k\ge 0\),  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7,
\]
establishing the optimality of the exponent in \((*)\).  In particular,
for \(k=0\) one obtains  

\[
\Delta(x)=\frac{(x-\pi)^{7}}{8}+O\!\bigl((x-\pi)^{8}\bigr),
\]
and the power \(7\) cannot be lowered.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Remarks.  

1.  If \(b_{1}\equiv b_{2}\equiv b_{3}\equiv 0\) then
\(k=+\infty\) and \(\Delta\equiv 0\); otherwise the estimate \((*)\) is
valid.  

2.  The explicit family (5) is only one concrete realisation with
equality.  Small analytic perturbations of any of the six functions by
terms of order \(\ge k+2\) keep the equality, so the set
\(\{\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7\}\) is Zariski-open in the space
of analytic sextuples.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem escalates from a single 2 × 2 expression to
   the determinant of a 3 × 3 matrix whose entries are integrals, introducing
   nine interacting objects instead of four.

2. Additional constraints:  Careful use of analytic expansions,
   column operations, and rank considerations are all required; simple
   differentiation no longer suffices.

3. Deeper structure:  The proof exploits subtle cancellations that arise
   only after eliminating leading terms in two entire columns, something that
   is invisible in the original 2 × 2 setting.

4. Multi-step reasoning:  One must combine Taylor expansions, linear-algebra
   manipulations that leave determinants invariant, and order bookkeeping to
   track five powers of (x–π).  Each part is elementary in isolation, but
   their coordination is substantially more sophisticated than in the
   original problem, where four successive derivatives were enough.

5. Non-trivial sharpness:  Showing that the exponent 5 is optimal in the
   generic case adds an extra theoretical layer beyond merely proving
   divisibility.

Collectively these enhancements push the problem well beyond the scope of
direct pattern-matching or routine calculations, satisfying the requirement
that the kernel variant be significantly harder than both earlier versions.

\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7 .
\]

Consequently, when \(k=0\) the determinant \(\Delta(x)\) is always
divisible by \((x-\pi)^{7}\), and the exponent \(7\) cannot be reduced.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

\[
\varepsilon:=x-\pi,\qquad 
\tau:=t-\pi,
\]
and use the \(O(\,\cdot\,)\)-notation component-wise for vectors and
matrices.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a) - Proof of the inequality \((*)\).

Step 0.  Taylor expansions.  
Write  

\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{j}(t)&=\tau^{k}\sum_{r\ge 0}\beta_{j,r}\,\tau^{r},
&\qquad& \beta_{1,0}\neq 0\;(\text{by }(\diamondsuit)),\\[2mm]
a_{i}(t)&=\sum_{s\ge 0}\alpha_{i,s}\,\tau^{s},
\qquad 1\le i\le 3.
\end{aligned}
\]
Put  

\[
s:=\min\Bigl\{s\ge 0\mid\exists\,i:\alpha_{i,s}\neq 0\Bigr\},\qquad 
\widehat u:=(\alpha_{1,s},\alpha_{2,s},\alpha_{3,s})^{\mathsf T}\neq 0 .
\]

Step 1.  The first column \(C_{1}\).  
For \(j=1\) the integrand is \(\tau^{k}\tau^{s}= \tau^{k+s}\); hence  

\[
C_{1}:=
\begin{bmatrix}m_{11}\\m_{21}\\m_{31}\end{bmatrix}
=\varepsilon^{k+1+s}\bigl(\widehat u+\varepsilon u_{1}+O(\varepsilon^{2})\bigr). \tag{1}
\]

Step 2.  Raising the order of the remaining columns.  
Put \(\lambda_{j}:=\beta_{j,0}/\beta_{1,0}\;(j=2,3)\) and perform the
elementary column operations \(C_{j}\longleftarrow C_{j}-\lambda_{j}C_{1}\).
Because \(b_{j}-\lambda_{j}b_{1}\) vanishes to order \(k+1\) at
\(t=\pi\), the transformed columns satisfy  

\[
C_{j}= \varepsilon^{k+2+s}\bigl(v_{j}+\varepsilon w_{j}+O(\varepsilon^{2})\bigr),
\qquad v_{j}=c_{j}\,\widehat u,\;c_{j}\in\mathbb{R}. \tag{2}
\]

Step 3.  Extracting the obvious overall power of \(\varepsilon\).  
With (1)-(2) we can factor  

\[
\Delta(x)=\varepsilon^{3k+5+3s}\,
B(\varepsilon),
\]
where  

\[
B(\varepsilon):=
\det\!\bigl[\widehat u+\varepsilon u_{1}+O(\varepsilon^{2}),
               \;v_{2}+\varepsilon w_{2}+O(\varepsilon^{2}),
               \;v_{3}+\varepsilon w_{3}+O(\varepsilon^{2})\bigr]. \tag{3}
\]

Step 4.  Vanishing of the constant and linear terms in \(B\).  
At \(\varepsilon=0\) the three column-vectors inside the determinant in
(3) are all multiples of \(\widehat u\), hence the determinant vanishes.  
Because each of the two vectors \(v_{2},v_{3}\) is a multiple of the
first one, every first-order term in the expansion of \(B(\varepsilon)\)
still contains two proportional columns and therefore also vanishes.
Consequently  

\[
B(\varepsilon)=\varepsilon^{2}\,B_{2}+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{3}\bigr) \qquad(\varepsilon\to 0). \tag{4}
\]

Inequalities (3)-(4) give  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta
\;\ge\;3k+5+3s+2
\;=\;3k+7+3s
\;\ge\;3k+7 ,
\]

which is exactly the universal estimate \((*)\).
(Note that when \(s>0\) the obtained bound is in fact stronger.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Optional remark on the coefficient \(B_{2}\).  
Formula (4) shows that \(\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta\) will jump to at
least \(3k+8+3s\) precisely when the scalar \(B_{2}\) vanishes.  One can
check that \(B_{2}\) is a non-trivial analytic function of the
coefficients \(\{\alpha_{i,r}\},\{\beta_{j,r}\}\); hence \(B_{2}=0\) is a
\emph{codimension-$1$} condition in the space of analytic sextuples.
For a ``generic'' choice of the six functions one therefore has
\(B_{2}\neq 0\) and the stronger bound
\(\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7+3s\).  This observation is not
needed for part (a), but it explains why the equality examples in part
(b) (which have \(s=0\)) do realise the exact exponent \(3k+7\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b) - Sharpness of the bound \((*)\).

Fix \(k\ge 0\) and take  

\[
\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
a_{1}(t)&:=1,\\
a_{2}(t)&:=1+\tau,\\
a_{3}(t)&:=\tau,\\
b_{1}(t)&:=\tau^{k},\\
b_{2}(t)&:=\tau^{k}\bigl(1+\tau\bigr),\\
b_{3}(t)&:=\tau^{k+1}\bigl(1+\tau\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{5}
\]
Here \(s=0\) and \(\min_{j}\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}b_{j}=k\); condition
\((\diamondsuit)\) is satisfied.

A straightforward (though routine) Taylor computation of the nine
integrals shows  

\[
\Delta(\pi+\varepsilon)=
\frac{k+3}{12(k+1)(k+2)}\,\varepsilon^{3k+7}
\,+\,O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{3k+8}\bigr),\qquad (\varepsilon\to 0). \tag{6}
\]
Since the leading coefficient in (6) is
\(\displaystyle\frac{k+3}{12(k+1)(k+2)}\neq 0\) for every \(k\ge 0\),  

\[
\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7,
\]
establishing the optimality of the exponent in \((*)\).  In particular,
for \(k=0\) one obtains  

\[
\Delta(x)=\frac{(x-\pi)^{7}}{8}+O\!\bigl((x-\pi)^{8}\bigr),
\]
and the power \(7\) cannot be lowered.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Remarks.  

1.  If \(b_{1}\equiv b_{2}\equiv b_{3}\equiv 0\) then
\(k=+\infty\) and \(\Delta\equiv 0\); otherwise the estimate \((*)\) is
valid.  

2.  The explicit family (5) is only one concrete realisation with
equality.  Small analytic perturbations of any of the six functions by
terms of order \(\ge k+2\) keep the equality, so the set
\(\{\operatorname{ord}_{\pi}\Delta=3k+7\}\) is Zariski-open in the space
of analytic sextuples.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem escalates from a single 2 × 2 expression to
   the determinant of a 3 × 3 matrix whose entries are integrals, introducing
   nine interacting objects instead of four.

2. Additional constraints:  Careful use of analytic expansions,
   column operations, and rank considerations are all required; simple
   differentiation no longer suffices.

3. Deeper structure:  The proof exploits subtle cancellations that arise
   only after eliminating leading terms in two entire columns, something that
   is invisible in the original 2 × 2 setting.

4. Multi-step reasoning:  One must combine Taylor expansions, linear-algebra
   manipulations that leave determinants invariant, and order bookkeeping to
   track five powers of (x–π).  Each part is elementary in isolation, but
   their coordination is substantially more sophisticated than in the
   original problem, where four successive derivatives were enough.

5. Non-trivial sharpness:  Showing that the exponent 5 is optimal in the
   generic case adds an extra theoretical layer beyond merely proving
   divisibility.

Collectively these enhancements push the problem well beyond the scope of
direct pattern-matching or routine calculations, satisfying the requirement
that the kernel variant be significantly harder than both earlier versions.
